meta.title: Confirm if an invitation is sent to a specific email in Partners Portal / Possibility to resend the invitation

tech_stack_fingerprints: GraphQL API (implied by other writeups from author)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can attempt to invite another user to the Partners Portal and there's a function to resend invitations.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account in the Facebook Partners Portal.
applicability_signals: Presence of an 'invite' functionality within the Partners Portal.
Existence of a 'resend invitation' option or a similar flow.
skip_if: No invitation or resend functionality is found.
All invitation attempts result in immediate, uninformative errors without confirmation or denial.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the Facebook Partners Portal with an authenticated user account.
Step 2: Navigate to sections related to user management, invitations, or team settings to identify invitation functionalities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to invite a target email address to the Partners Portal. Observe the response for any indication of success or failure.

Step 2: If an invitation can be sent, locate and use the 'resend invitation' functionality for the same target email. Observe if the response differs or if there's a confirmation that an invitation was already sent.
Step 3: If the system provides feedback on whether an invitation was sent or not (e.g., 'invitation already sent', 'invalid email'), this confirms the disclosure.
Step 4: If direct confirmation is not available, attempt to send invitations to known emails and unknown emails to discern different response patterns.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses related to the invitation and resend invitation functionalities.
success_indicators: A distinct response from the server confirming that an invitation has already been sent to the specified email address.
A different response when attempting to resend an invitation compared to sending a new one for a non-existent invite.
The ability to determine, with high confidence, whether a specific email address has received an invitation without prior knowledge.
failure_indicators: Consistent generic error messages for all email addresses (sent, not sent, already exists).
No discernible difference in responses between sending a new invitation and resending an existing one.
The system does not provide any feedback regarding the status of an invitation for a given email.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can confirm whether a specific email address has been invited to the Facebook Partners Portal, potentially aiding in further social engineering or reconnaissance efforts. This information could be used to infer user activity or presence within the platform.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for information disclosure vulnerabilities in invitation and notification systems, especially when actions like resending are available, as they can reveal the state of an entity (e.g., whether an invitation exists).

meta.title: XSS in Facebook CDN due to improper filtering of uploaded files extensions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can upload files to a Facebook CDN and these files' extensions are not properly filtered, leading to script execution.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to upload files that are then served through the Facebook CDN.
applicability_signals: Endpoints related to file uploads (e.g., media upload API).
Responses that indicate successful file upload and provide a URL for the uploaded file.
Observation of how the uploaded files are served (e.g., through a CDN).
skip_if: All uploaded file extensions are strictly validated and only whitelisted types are allowed.
Uploaded files are always served with `Content-Disposition: attachment` and `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` headers, preventing script execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify file upload functionalities within Facebook (e.g., for profile pictures, posts, messages).
Step 2: Attempt to upload various file types, including those with potentially executable extensions like `.html`, `.js`, or files disguised with multiple extensions (e.g., `image.jpg.html`).
Step 3: Analyze the URLs provided for uploaded files to understand the CDN structure and how files are served.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a file with an extension that might bypass filtering (e.g., `exploit.html`).
Step 2: If the upload is successful, obtain the URL of the uploaded file from the CDN.
Step 3: Craft a payload within the uploaded file (e.g., an HTML file containing JavaScript to trigger XSS).
Step 4: Share the CDN URL of the crafted file with a victim.
payloads_or_patterns: <html><body><script>alert('XSS: <USER_ID>')</script></body></html> (uploaded as a file with a bypass extension)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To test different file extensions and observe response behavior.
Custom script: To automate file uploads and URL generation.
success_indicators: The XSS payload executes in the victim's browser when they access the shared CDN URL.
Browser console shows JavaScript errors related to content type or execution blockers (indicating a potential bypass was attempted).
failure_indicators: File upload fails with an error message indicating unsupported file type.
Uploaded file is served with incorrect Content-Type header, preventing script execution.
Uploaded file is automatically sanitized or rewritten, removing the malicious payload.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of users who access the crafted file, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, or further account compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Improper filtering of uploaded file extensions on CDNs can lead to XSS vulnerabilities; always validate and sanitize file types at the server-side before serving them.

meta.title: Enumerate internal cached URLs which lead to data exposure

tech_stack_fingerprints: Likely uses a CDN for content delivery

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can influence or observe cached resources, especially those potentially containing internal or sensitive information.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that serve or reference cached content.
Features that involve content delivery or sharing where caching might be involved.
Discovery of any URL patterns that seem related to internal resources or assets.
skip_if: All observed cached URLs are confirmed to be public and non-sensitive.
WAF blocks requests attempting to access a wide range of URLs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that serve or reference cached content. Look for parameters that might control cache keys or resource identifiers.
Step 2: Analyze responses for clues about how URLs are constructed for cached assets, especially looking for patterns that might indicate internal paths or unlinked resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access a variety of URLs that might be internally cached. This could involve guessing common internal file paths, administrative interfaces, or specific asset types.
Step 2: Observe responses for any indication of whether the URL was successfully retrieved from cache and if it reveals information that should not be public.
Step 3: If specific patterns are identified in Step 1 of recon, try enumerating variations of those patterns to discover more cached resources.
payloads_or_patterns: Construct URLs targeting potential internal paths or known asset types, e.g., /<internal_path>/resource.js, /assets/admin_config.json, /user_data/<USER_ID>/profile.cache
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder for enumerating URL patterns and parameters.
Custom scripts for generating and testing large numbers of URLs.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response for a URL that should not be publicly accessible.
Response content reveals internal paths, configuration details, sensitive metadata, or unlinked user data.
Observed differences in response headers or content for cached vs. non-cached versions of a resource, indicating successful cache hit.
failure_indicators: All attempted URLs result in 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden errors. Suggest trying different URL patterns or focusing on specific features known to use caching.
Responses are identical for all attempted URLs, suggesting a lack of dynamic content or effective caching of public assets only. Pivot to other attack vectors.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive internal data, configuration files, unlinked user information, or administrative interfaces that are not intended for public access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Enumerate internal URL patterns and asset types within cached responses to discover exposed sensitive information that is not directly linked.

meta.title: Make recruiting referrals on behalf of employees

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can create recruiting referrals, and the system does not properly validate that the referral is being made by the legitimate employee.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: Presence of a feature to create recruiting referrals.
API endpoints related to referral creation that may accept employee IDs or names as parameters.
skip_if: Referral creation requires explicit employee association that cannot be manipulated.
Referral creation process involves strong multi-factor authentication tied to the employee.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the recruiting section of Facebook to identify the referral creation interface.

Step 2: Use browser developer tools to monitor network requests during the referral creation process to identify relevant API endpoints and parameters, especially those related to the referring employee.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the process of creating a recruiting referral as a logged-in attacker.

Step 2: During the referral creation process, attempt to manipulate parameters that specify the referring employee. This might involve trying to substitute the attacker's own employee ID or the ID of another employee.
Step 3: Submit the referral with the manipulated employee identifier.
payloads_or_patterns: GraphQL query or REST API call targeting referral creation, with a manipulated `employee_id` or `referrer_id` parameter, e.g., `mutation { createReferral(input: { candidate_email: "<CANDIDATE_EMAIL>", employee_id: "<TARGET_EMPLOYEE_ID>" }) { ... } }`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify referral creation requests, specifically targeting employee identifiers.
success_indicators: The system successfully creates a recruiting referral associated with the targeted employee, even though the attacker is not that employee.
Notifications or actions related to the referral are sent to the targeted employee or recruiters, indicating the referral was made on their behalf.
failure_indicators: API returns an error indicating invalid `employee_id` or lack of authorization to refer on behalf of another employee.
Referral creation fails and the original authenticated user's employee ID is enforced.
Referral is created but correctly attributed to the attacker's own employee account.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can make recruiting referrals on behalf of any employee, potentially impersonating them in the recruitment process, sending unsolicited referrals, or abusing the referral system for malicious purposes.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the authenticated user creating a referral is the same user whose identity is being used as the referrer.

meta.title: Leaking Facebook user information to external websites / Setting some cookies values

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Facebook allows certain user-related information (like cookies or user IDs) to be exposed or transmitted to external domains, potentially through cross-site scripting or insecure direct object references.
prerequisites: No specific prerequisites mentioned beyond the target system's susceptibility to the described information leakage.
applicability_signals: Observing Facebook sending sensitive cookies or user identifiers to third-party domains without proper validation.
The writeup mentions the ability to set cookie values that are then leaked to external websites.
skip_if: All outgoing requests from Facebook to external sites are properly sanitized and do not include sensitive user information.
Facebook implements robust SameSite cookie policies and Content Security Policies that prevent unauthorized cross-site cookie access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic when interacting with Facebook features that involve third-party integrations or redirects to identify how user information or cookies are transmitted.
Step 2: Use browser developer tools to inspect cookies and their attributes (domain, path, SameSite policy) set by Facebook.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a Facebook endpoint or feature that is vulnerable to setting or leaking cookie values to external domains.

Step 2: Craft a request or trigger an action that causes Facebook to set a specific cookie or transmit sensitive user information in a way that it becomes accessible to an attacker-controlled external website.
Step 3: For cookie leakage, an attacker would control an external website and observe if specific Facebook cookies are sent to it in the `Referer` header or other parts of the HTTP request.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup for specific payloads, but likely involves manipulating requests to trigger cookie setting or leakage.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses, identify cookie-related vulnerabilities.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting cookies, network traffic, and JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: An attacker-controlled external website receives Facebook user information (e.g., cookies like `c_user`, `xs`, or user IDs) in the HTTP request headers or response.
The ability to confirm specific cookie values being leaked to a third-party domain.
failure_indicators: No sensitive Facebook cookies or user information are observed on the attacker-controlled external website.
Facebook's security mechanisms (e.g., HttpOnly, SameSite attributes for cookies, Content Security Policy) prevent the leakage.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive Facebook user information, including session cookies, to external websites. This could lead to account hijacking, identity theft, and further exploitation of user accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify how sensitive cookies and user identifiers are handled when interacting with third-party services or when data is transmitted across different domains. Ensure that such data is never exposed to unauthorized parties.

meta.title: Access private information about SparkAR effect owners who has a publicly viewable portfolio

failure_indicators: Only publicly intended information is visible in the portfolio, no private details are exposed. Pivot to other vulnerability categories if applicable.

meta.title: Open redirect in Instagram.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When parameters in Instagram URLs appear to be unvalidated or can be manipulated to redirect users to external sites.
prerequisites: The target's domain is instagram.com or a subdomain thereof.
applicability_signals: URLs containing parameters that accept external URLs or hostnames.
Observing redirects after clicking links that include URL parameters.
skip_if: All tested URL parameters are properly validated against a whitelist of internal domains.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks requests containing common open redirect payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse instagram.com and identify any URLs that contain query parameters which might accept a URL or hostname.
Step 2: Analyze the behavior of these URLs, specifically looking for redirects after clicking on them or observing the Network tab in browser developer tools for redirect responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL by appending a suspicious parameter (e.g., 'redirect_uri', 'url', 'next') with a value pointing to an attacker-controlled domain.
Step 2: Send this crafted URL to the victim.
Step 3: If the victim clicks the link and is redirected to the attacker's domain, the vulnerability is confirmed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Repeater or Intruder to systematically test various parameters and payloads for open redirect vulnerabilities.
Browser Developer Tools: Monitor network traffic for redirect responses (301, 302) and analyze URL parameters.
success_indicators: The user is redirected to an external domain specified in the crafted URL, instead of remaining on instagram.com or a legitimate Instagram page.
The response code for the crafted URL is a redirect (e.g., 302 Found) to the attacker-controlled domain.
failure_indicators: The user remains on instagram.com or is redirected to an unexpected, but still instagram.com-related, page. → Try other URL parameters or different redirection parameters like 'goto', 'returnTo'.
A 403 Forbidden or other error response is received. → The parameter or path might be protected by a WAF or require specific authentication.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick users into visiting malicious websites by crafting links that appear to originate from instagram.com, leading to phishing attacks, credential theft, or malware distribution.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate external URLs provided in query parameters against a strict whitelist of legitimate internal domains to prevent open redirect vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Hunting for bugs in Telegram's animated stickers remote attack surface

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of animated stickers feature in Telegram app
Usage of rlottie library (forked by Telegram)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user receives or attempts to preview an animated sticker sent by an attacker.
prerequisites: The target user must be running a vulnerable version of the Telegram client.
The attacker must be able to send an animated sticker to the target user.
applicability_signals: Telegram supports animated stickers.
The application uses a native library (rlottie) for parsing and rendering these animations.
skip_if: The target user has updated their Telegram client to a patched version.
The attacker cannot successfully send a crafted animated sticker to the target.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the rlottie library's source code (if available or reverse engineer) for potential memory corruption vulnerabilities, focusing on parsing and rendering logic for Lottie JSON files.
Step 2: Research common Lottie animation structures and properties that could lead to edge cases in native library parsing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Lottie JSON file that targets a specific memory corruption vulnerability identified in the rlottie library (e.g., heap out-of-bounds write, stack out-of-bounds read/write, integer overflow).
Step 2: Package this malicious JSON into an animated sticker.
Step 3: Send the crafted animated sticker to a target user.
Step 4: Observe for application crashes (DoS) or potential remote code execution if vulnerabilities allow.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted Lottie JSON files designed to trigger specific memory corruption flaws in the rlottie library. Example: A JSON with extremely large values for certain animation properties, malformed structures, or invalid data types.
tools_recommended: Fuzzing tools (e.g., AFL++, libFuzzer) for generating malformed Lottie files.
Debuggers (e.g., GDB, LLDB) for analyzing crashes and understanding memory corruption.
Hex editors for manual crafting of payloads.
Reverse engineering tools (e.g., IDA Pro, Ghidra) to understand the rlottie library's internal workings.
success_indicators: Target Telegram application crashes (Denial of Service).

If a heap/stack out-of-bounds write occurs and can be controlled, potential for arbitrary code execution (though not explicitly demonstrated in the writeup as a direct attack outcome, it's the underlying risk of the identified memory corruption).
Observing application behavior that deviates from normal operation after processing the sticker.
failure_indicators: The animated sticker is processed without any noticeable issues or crashes.
The Telegram client correctly sanitizes or rejects the malformed Lottie data, preventing the vulnerability from being triggered.
A 4xx/5xx error if the sticker upload or sending mechanism itself fails due to basic validation.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (application crash) for any user who receives and attempts to view a specially crafted animated sticker. Potentially, a successful exploitation of heap out-of-bounds write could lead to remote code execution, allowing an attacker to compromise the user's device.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize native libraries used for parsing complex, potentially untrusted input formats (like JSON-based animations) for memory corruption vulnerabilities, as these can lead to significant security impacts on mobile applications.

meta.title: SHAREit Flaw Could Lead to Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a vulnerable version of the SHAREit Android application is installed on a target device and the application is vulnerable to insecure content provider access or intent handling.
prerequisites: Target device must have a vulnerable version of SHAREit installed.
An attacker must be able to interact with the target device, likely through a Man-in-the-Disk (MITD) attack or by manipulating files SHAREit accesses.
applicability_signals: The SHAREit application uses a Content Provider that is not properly secured.
The SHAREit application handles intents in a way that can be exploited by malicious applications or manipulated data.
skip_if: The target device has an updated and patched version of SHAREit installed.
Android security features (e.g., SELinux, strict mode) are configured to prevent unauthorized access to content providers or inter-app communication.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of SHAREit installed on the target device. This can be done through app information or by analyzing app manifests if the APK is available.

Step 2: Analyze the SHAREit Android application's Content Provider definitions and Intent filter configurations to identify potential vulnerabilities, such as exported components without proper permissions or insecure data handling.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious application or manipulate files on the device to interact with SHAREit's vulnerable Content Provider. This might involve writing malicious data to a file that SHAREit processes.
Step 2: Trigger the vulnerable functionality within SHAREit by either making a direct request to the Content Provider or by initiating an Intent that the application mishandles.

Step 3: Exploit the mishandled data or Intent to execute arbitrary code on the device. This could involve overwriting critical application files or injecting malicious code through a vulnerable data parsing mechanism.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. Actual RCE payload would depend on the specific vulnerability and exploit chain.
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (ADB) for interacting with devices.
MobSF (Mobile Security Framework) for static and dynamic analysis of the SHAREit APK.
Frida for dynamic instrumentation and hooking.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the target device.
Installation of malicious code or processes.
Demonstrable compromise of the target device's security.
failure_indicators: Application crashes without executing malicious code.
Android system prevents the malicious action (e.g., permission denied errors).
No observable change in device behavior indicating compromise.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the target device, allowing an attacker to take full control of the user's device, steal sensitive data, install malware, or use the device for further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize exported Android Content Providers and Intent handling mechanisms for improper permission checks and insecure data parsing that could lead to code execution.

meta.title: Access files uploaded by employees to internal CDNs / Regenerate URL signature of user uploaded content.

affected_components: User uploaded content URLs on internal CDNs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload content or generate URLs for user-uploaded content that is hosted on an internal CDN, and these URLs might have signatures or access controls that can be bypassed or regenerated.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Facebook account.
Attacker has a way to upload content or obtain URLs for user-uploaded content.
applicability_signals: Observation of URLs for user-uploaded content containing signature parameters.
User-uploaded files are hosted on a CDN domain controlled by Facebook.
skip_if: All user-uploaded content URLs are publicly accessible without any form of signature or access control.
The CDN returns a 404 or 403 error for any URL that does not have a valid, current signature.

recon_steps: Step 1: Upload a file through a legitimate user action (e.g., profile picture, post image) on Facebook.
Step 2: Observe the generated URL for the uploaded file. Identify if it contains a signature or token parameter.
Step 3: Attempt to access the file using the observed URL with the same session to confirm it's accessible.
Step 4: Try modifying parts of the URL, especially potential signature components, to see if access is still granted or if error messages provide clues.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a valid URL for a user-uploaded file from an internal Facebook CDN.
Step 2: Analyze the URL structure to identify components that might be part of a signature (e.g., timestamps, random strings, checksums).

Step 3: Attempt to manipulate or regenerate these signature components. This might involve: Guessing common signature formats, Replaying old signatures, Trying to use current time-based signatures, or Exploiting predictable generation logic.
Step 4: Test the modified URL to see if it grants access to the file, potentially bypassing intended access controls or time limits.
payloads_or_patterns: Example URL structure with potential signature: <TARGET_URL>/<path>/<filename>.<ext>?signature=<SIGNATURE_PARAM>&expires=<TIMESTAMP_PARAM>
Payload pattern: Attempt to increment/decrement <TIMESTAMP_PARAM> by small intervals (e.g., 1 second, 1 minute) or guess common signature formats.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To systematically test variations of signature parameters.
Custom script: To automate the generation and testing of signature parameters based on observed patterns.
success_indicators: Successfully accessing a user-uploaded file using a manipulated or regenerated URL, especially if the original URL has expired or should be inaccessible.
Receiving a 200 OK response with the file content when using a forged signature.
Accessing files uploaded by other users by regenerating their file URLs.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found response for all manipulated URLs, indicating strong signature validation and access control.
Signature validation fails consistently, returning specific error messages that don't reveal logic flaws.

playbooks.impact: Access to user-uploaded files hosted on internal CDNs, potentially including sensitive information or media, even if their original access links have expired or were intended to be private.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with user-uploaded content on CDNs, always investigate the URL structure for signature or token parameters and attempt to manipulate or regenerate them to bypass access controls.

meta.title: Full account takeover worth $1000 Think out of the box

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows authenticated users to change their password without validating the current password and uses a predictable user ID (e.g., 6-digit numeric) that can be discovered or enumerated.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session on the target application.
User ID is discoverable or guessable (e.g., numeric, sequential).
applicability_signals: Password change functionality allows submission without current password.
User ID can be found in HTML source or API responses.
Param Miner can identify a 'uid' parameter in JSON requests for password changes.
skip_if: Password change requires current password validation.
User IDs are not guessable or discoverable.
WAF blocks enumeration attempts on user IDs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in with an authenticated account and navigate to account settings.
Step 2: Observe HTTP requests for changes to user information (email, name) and password changes. Look for CSRF tokens and the inclusion of user identifiers.
Step 3: View page source to identify how user IDs are assigned and if they are sequential or guessable.
Step 4: Use Burp Suite's Param Miner extension with 'Guess JSON parameter' to identify hidden parameters in JSON request bodies for password change functionality.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password change request from the attacker's account, but without providing the current password.
Step 2: If the previous step fails, capture a request where a password change is attempted and remove the CSRF token.

Step 3: If CSRF tokens prevent the above, then attempt to change the password by sending a request to the password change endpoint, but instead of using the attacker's user ID, use a discovered or guessed user ID of another user (e.g., from Param Miner).
Step 4: Modify the password change request JSON body to include the target user's ID in a parameter identified by Param Miner (e.g., 'uid').
payloads_or_patterns:
POST <TARGET_URL>/change_password HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"new_password": "<NEW_PASSWORD>", "uid": "<TARGET_USER_ID>"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests.
Burp Suite Param Miner extension: To discover hidden parameters, especially in JSON bodies.
success_indicators: The password for the target user is changed successfully.
The attacker can log in to the target user's account using the newly set password.
The response indicates a successful password update without requiring the old password and with the correct target user ID.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or other error response indicating insufficient permissions.
Password change fails due to requiring the current password validation.
Response indicates the user ID parameter is invalid or not found. (Pivot: try other discovered parameters or enumeration techniques).

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of any user, allowing the attacker to access and control their account, including sensitive information and actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test password reset and change functionality for weaknesses, especially the absence of current password validation and the possibility of substituting user identifiers within the request payload.

meta.title: Delete linked payments accounts of a Facebook page (or user)

affected_components: payments account linking feature for pages or users

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can initiate the process of linking or unlinking payment accounts for a Facebook page or their own user account.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: Presence of UI elements or API endpoints related to managing payment account links.
Ability to view or modify linked payment accounts through the Facebook interface or API.
skip_if: All actions related to payment account management are strictly tied to the owner of the page/account and cannot be manipulated by other authenticated users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to a Facebook account and navigate to the settings or payment section of a Facebook page or user profile.
Step 2: Identify the functionality for linking or unlinking payment accounts. Observe the HTTP requests made when performing these actions, noting any unique identifiers or parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to find an API endpoint or functionality that allows unlinking payment accounts.

Step 2: If a direct unlinking endpoint is found, attempt to send a request to unlink a payment account associated with a target page/user by manipulating the request parameters (e.g., page ID, payment account ID, user ID) with the attacker's own authenticated session.

Step 3: If direct unlinking is not possible, explore if there are any IDOR vulnerabilities in the process of linking payment accounts. For example, if a user can link a payment account to their profile, check if they can provide the ID of another user's profile or page to link the payment account to.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/vX/unlink_payment_account HTTP/1.1
Host: www.facebook.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"page_id": "<TARGET_PAGE_ID>", "payment_account_id": "<TARGET_PAYMENT_ACCOUNT_ID>"}

POST /api/vX/link_payment_account HTTP/1.1
Host: www.facebook.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"target_profile_id": "<TARGET_USER_OR_PAGE_ID>", "payment_account_details": "..."}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests related to payment account management. Use Repeater to test different parameters and session tokens.
success_indicators: The targeted Facebook page or user no longer has the specified payment account linked.
The attacker receives a success response (e.g., 200 OK) from the server after attempting to unlink the payment account.
Error messages indicating permission denied or invalid parameters suggest the endpoint exists but authorization checks are in place.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or similar authorization error when attempting to modify payment accounts not belonging to the attacker.
404 Not Found or unexpected error if the endpoint does not exist or is misconfigured.
No change in the linked payment accounts despite sending requests.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can unlink legitimate payment accounts from a Facebook page or user, potentially disrupting services, causing financial loss, or enabling further account manipulation if payment methods are critical for account recovery or verification.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that actions involving sensitive linked resources (like payment accounts) are strictly restricted to the resource owner, and test for IDOR by attempting to manipulate resource identifiers belonging to other users or pages.

meta.title: URLs in img tag aren’t passed through safe_image.php which lead to exposure of Facebook users IPs.

Identifying the `safe_image.php` script in network requests or source code.
skip_if: All image URLs are properly sanitized and validated by `safe_image.php`.
`safe_image.php` does not appear to be in use for image processing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse Facebook pages and profiles to identify instances where user-generated content might include `` tags.
Step 2: Inspect network traffic for requests to `safe_image.php` when images are loaded, to confirm its usage and identify parameters.
Step 3: Analyze the source code of relevant Facebook pages for how `` tags are generated and processed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL that, when loaded by `safe_image.php`, reveals the IP address of the user viewing it.
Step 2: Inject this crafted URL into an `` tag within a Facebook feature that allows user-generated content.
Step 3: Observe if the server logs or error messages associated with `safe_image.php` execution reveal the IP address of the viewer.
payloads_or_patterns: The writeup doesn't provide a concrete payload but implies that a crafted URL could be used. A potential approach might involve a URL that triggers a server-side request or an error that logs the client IP. Example concept: `` where `attacker-controlled-server` logs the incoming IP.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze network requests, and potentially set up a collaborator client to receive IP addresses.
success_indicators: The attacker receives an IP address of a Facebook user in their logs or in a response from `safe_image.php`.
The `safe_image.php` script attempts to fetch a resource from an attacker-controlled server, and the attacker's server receives a request containing the victim's IP address.
failure_indicators: The `safe_image.php` script correctly sanitizes the URL and does not fetch external resources or log the user's IP.
The `safe_image.php` script returns a generic error without revealing any user-specific information.

meta.title: Leak of internal categorySets names and employees test accounts.

Endpoints that list or describe internal categories or sets.

Step 2: Investigate endpoints that return lists of categories, sets, or internal configurations. Look for parameters that might be used for filtering, sorting, or pagination.
Step 3: Attempt to enumerate 'test accounts' by observing patterns in user IDs or by looking for specific user types in API responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send requests to identified endpoints with variations in parameters to attempt to access or list internal categorySets.
Step 2: If employee test accounts are suspected, try to guess or enumerate potential account identifiers based on common naming conventions or patterns observed in other user data.
Step 3: Analyze responses for sensitive information such as internal category names, descriptions, and identifiers for employee test accounts.

API responses revealing identifiers or information about employee test accounts.

meta.title: View orders and financial reports lists for any page shop

meta.title: How I Hacked Everyone’s Resume/CV’s and Got €€€

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can upload and view their CV/resume on a job search portal, and the associated endpoint is susceptible to IDOR.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target portal.
applicability_signals: The existence of endpoints related to CV/resume upload or retrieval.
Receiving an email containing profile information that includes an endpoint for the CV/resume.
skip_if: The CV/resume endpoints consistently return 404 or 403 for all users, or require specific authorization headers not obtainable by a standard authenticated user.

Step 2: Upon receiving an email with profile information, extract any URLs or endpoints mentioned, particularly those related to CVs or resumes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the identified CV/resume endpoint (e.g., `https://www.target.com/profile/cv/<user_id>`) using the obtained URL.

Step 2: If the endpoint does not require specific user session tokens within the URL and appears to be directly accessible, attempt to enumerate or guess `user_id` parameters to access other users' CVs.
Step 3: Observe the response for any indication of another user's CV/resume data.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To systematically test a range of `USER_ID` values against the `/profile/cv/` endpoint.
Manual Browser Inspection: To analyze the structure of responses and identify potential `USER_ID` patterns or lack thereof.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response with another user's CV/resume content when accessing `https://www.target.com/profile/cv/<OTHER_USER_ID>`.
Any response that contains identifiable data belonging to a user other than the authenticated user.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found for all attempted `USER_ID` values. This may indicate that the endpoint is properly secured or that the ID format is different (e.g., UUIDs instead of sequential numbers).
If the endpoint requires additional authentication headers or parameters that cannot be bypassed, the IDOR is not exploitable via this direct URL method.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can access and download the resumes/CVs of all users on the job search portal, leading to mass sensitive information disclosure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check if user-specific resource URLs are predictable and lack proper authorization checks, especially for sensitive documents like resumes, after obtaining an authenticated session.

meta.title: Changing other users Episode title & description - IDOR Vulnerability in [REDACTED]

target_profile.technology: Unknown, but appears to be a web application managing podcast episodes.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access and modify their own podcast episode metadata, and the episode ID is exposed and guessable.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: Endpoints for editing episode metadata exist.
Episode IDs are exposed in URLs or API responses.
Episode IDs are predictable (e.g., alphanumeric strings that can be enumerated).
skip_if: Episode IDs are GUIDs or cannot be enumerated.
API requests for metadata modification are properly authorized and enforce ownership checks.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the target application as a regular user.
Step 2: Navigate to the section for managing podcast episodes.
Step 3: Observe the URL structure when accessing or editing an episode's metadata. Identify the episode ID.

Step 4: Examine API requests made during episode editing (e.g., using browser developer tools or Burp Suite) to identify the endpoint and method used for metadata updates. Confirm the episode ID is present in the request.
attack_steps: Step 1: As User A, access the metadata edit page for one of your own episodes (e.g., `https://<TARGET_URL>/dashboard/episode/<ATTACKER_USER_ID>/metadata/edit`).

Step 2: Capture the HTTP request made when saving changes to the episode metadata (e.g., using Burp Suite Repeater). This will likely be a PUT or POST request to an API endpoint like `/api/podcastepisode/<EPISODE_ID>/metadata`.
Step 3: Identify the victim's episode ID. The writeup states that episode IDs are visible to anyone, implying they can be found by observing other users' public episode pages or through enumeration.

Step 4: In Burp Suite Repeater, modify the captured request. Change the `episode_id` in the URL path to the victim's episode ID (e.g., from `/api/podcastepisode/e3mid0/metadata` to `/api/podcastepisode/e3mhq4/metadata`).
Step 5: Modify other fields in the request body (e.g., `title`, `description`) to desired values.
Step 6: Send the modified request.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Proxy)
success_indicators: HTTP status code 200 OK received for the modified request.
Verification by navigating to the victim's episode page and observing that the title and description have been changed to the attacker's specified values.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized response: Indicates proper authorization checks are in place. Attempt to enumerate different ID formats if available.
404 Not Found: Episode ID might be incorrect or the endpoint structure is different. Double-check ID and endpoint.
No change observed on victim's page: The modification might have failed server-side or is not reflected immediately. Re-check request and response, consider cache.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can modify the title and description of any podcast episode belonging to other users, potentially defacing content, spreading misinformation, or impersonating other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the authenticated user making a request to modify an object is the authorized owner of that object, even if object IDs are discoverable.

meta.title: [GITLAB] — Server Side Request Forgery in “Project Import” page.

tech_stack_fingerprints: The presence of a 'Project Import' feature, specifically 'Import Project - Repo by URL'.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when GitLab's Project Import feature allows users to specify a repository URL and the server follows redirects when fetching the repository.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on GitLab.
Attacker must have control over a web server that can host a redirecting PHP script.
applicability_signals: GitLab's Project Import feature accepts external URLs.
The server-side logic for fetching repositories follows HTTP redirects.
skip_if: GitLab explicitly blocks all redirect URLs.
The Project Import feature does not accept external repository URLs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the 'Project Import' page in GitLab.
Step 2: Select the 'Repo by URL' option.
Step 3: Observe if the form accepts external URLs and if there are any immediate validation errors.
Step 4: Attempt to provide a URL pointing to a known internal IP (e.g., http://127.0.0.1) to see if it's blocked directly.
Step 5: If direct internal IPs are blocked, research common bypasses for localhost notation (octal, hex, decimal variants).
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a PHP script on a controlled VPS that sets a `Location` header to an internal target (e.g., `http://127.0.0.1:1339`). The script content should be: `<?php header("Location: http://127.0.0.1:1339"); ?>`.
Step 2: On the GitLab server, start a listener on the target internal port (e.g., `nc -lvvp 1339`).
Step 3: In GitLab's 'Project Import' page, provide the URL of the redirecting PHP script as the repository URL (e.g., `http://<YOUR_VPS_IP>/redirect.php`).
Step 4: Click 'Create Project' and observe the listener for incoming connections.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze the HTTP requests and responses during the import process. Useful for testing different redirect statuses.
Netcat (nc): To set up listeners on internal ports to detect incoming connections from the SSRF.
success_indicators: A connection is established on the `nc` listener, indicating that the GitLab server successfully made a request to the internal target specified in the PHP redirect script.
The listener shows the `Host` header as `localhost:<TARGET_PORT>` and the `User-Agent` as `git/...`.
failure_indicators: The import process fails with an error message indicating an invalid URL or repository.
No connection is received on the `nc` listener after initiating the project import.
GitLab's server returns a 403 Forbidden error for the redirect URL, suggesting WAF or strict URL filtering.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can leverage this SSRF vulnerability to access internal GitLab services, scan internal ports, and potentially interact with other internal applications, bypassing network restrictions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with import or external resource fetching features, always test for SSRF vulnerabilities, paying close attention to how redirects are handled and if localhost bypasses (octal, hex, decimal) are effective.

meta.title: How I was able to get extra coins

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a mobile application's logic can be bypassed by dynamically altering function return values to grant access to features or rewards that should be restricted or time-gated.
prerequisites: Rooted Android device
Ability to decompile the application's APK
Understanding of the target application's Java/Kotlin code
applicability_signals: Application exhibits a feature that grants a reward or access only once per 24 hours.
Decompilation reveals a function responsible for checking the eligibility for the reward, which returns a boolean value.
The target function's logic can be identified and understood.
skip_if: Application uses robust anti-tampering or anti-debugging mechanisms that prevent Frida from hooking.
The reward logic is implemented server-side and not dependent on client-side boolean checks.
The application is not installable on a rooted device or Frida server cannot be run.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download the target Android application's APK file.
Step 2: Decompile the APK using a tool like jadx to analyze the application's source code.
Step 3: Search the decompiled code for keywords related to the feature (e.g., 'daily bonus', 'coins', 'claim', 'available').
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the specific function within the decompiled code that controls the availability or claiming of the daily bonus (e.g., 'shouldShowDailyBonus()').
Step 2: Analyze the return logic of this function to understand what determines its outcome (true/false).
Step 3: Prepare a Frida script to hook this function and force its return value to 'true' (or the desired value that grants the bonus).
Step 4: Start the Frida server on a rooted Android device.
Step 5: Connect to the target application using Frida with the prepared script.
Step 6: Trigger the daily bonus feature within the application.
Step 7: Observe if the bonus is successfully claimed and extra coins are awarded.
payloads_or_patterns:
Java.perform(function() { var classA = Java.use("com.game.class.name"); classA.shouldShowDailyBonus.implementation = function(x) { console.log("In function shouldShowDailyBonus"); return True; }; });
frida -U --no-pause -l <JAVASCRIPT_FILE_NAME>.js -f <TARGET_APP_PACKAGE_NAME>
tools_recommended: jadx: For decompiling APK files.
Frida: For dynamic instrumentation and function hooking.
adb: For managing Android devices and pushing files.
success_indicators: The daily bonus claim button becomes active and can be clicked.
Coins are successfully added to the user's account beyond the normal daily limit.
The application does not detect the instrumentation or crash.
failure_indicators: The daily bonus cannot be claimed, and the same restrictions apply (e.g., 'claim again in 24 hours' message).
The application crashes or closes after running Frida.

Frida reports errors like 'unable to find module for', suggesting the target class/function might be obfuscated or not present as expected. Pivot: Try obfuscation-aware hooking techniques or look for server-side logic.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can repeatedly claim daily bonuses, obtaining a large number of in-game coins without waiting or paying real money. This can lead to an unfair advantage in the game, potentially allowing the attacker to access premium features or dominate leaderboards.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze client-side logic for features that grant rewards or access, as these may be bypassable by dynamically altering function return values or application state using tools like Frida.

meta.title: The "P" in Telegram stands for Privacy

Step 2: Send a self-destructing audio or video message to the target.
Step 3: Observe if the sent media message persists locally on the sender's device even after the self-destruct timer expires and the message disappears from the chat.

payloads_or_patterns: N/A (no specific exploit payload, but observation of file system activity)

meta.title: An Accidental XSS on uu.nl

tech_stack_fingerprints: Blogger platform (implied by the blogging platform)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application reflects user-controlled input within HTML title tags without proper sanitization, and the input can be controlled via a URL parameter.
prerequisites: Discovery of a URL parameter that reflects user input into the HTML title tag.
applicability_signals: A URL parameter (e.g., 'uuid') that, when modified, causes its value to be reflected within the <title> tag of the HTML response.
skip_if: User input is properly sanitized and does not allow for HTML tag injection.
The application uses Content Security Policy (CSP) that prevents inline script execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains of uu.nl using tools like `subfinder` or `assetfinder`.
Step 2: Use `waybackurls` on discovered subdomains to collect historical URLs.
Step 3: Analyze collected URLs for patterns indicating reflective parameters, particularly those appearing in title tags.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a target URL with a reflective parameter, e.g., `https://www.<target_subdomain>.uu.nl/XXXXXX/?uuid=<value>`.
Step 2: Craft a payload to break out of the title tag and inject a script: `test</title><script>alert(document.domain)</script>`.
Step 3: Append the payload to the identified reflective parameter: `https://www.<target_subdomain>.uu.nl/XXXXXX/?uuid=test</title><script>alert(document.domain)</script>`.
Step 4: Access the crafted URL in a web browser.
tools_recommended: subfinder: For subdomain enumeration.
waybackurls: For collecting historical URLs.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying the domain name (e.g., `www.<target_subdomain>.uu.nl`).
The injected JavaScript code is executed in the browser.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and the injected script is not rendered or executed.
The payload is escaped or filtered by the server-side or client-side.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is returned, indicating WAF interference.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session on the uu.nl domain, allowing for session hijacking, credential theft, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test reflective parameters in HTML title tags for XSS vulnerabilities by attempting to break out of the tag and inject script payloads.

meta.title: Microsoft Remote Desktop Web Access Authentication Timing Attack

target_profile.technology: Microsoft Remote Desktop Web Access (RD WA)
tech_stack_fingerprints: RD Web Access login page presence

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When RD Web Access is exposed to the internet and attackers can send numerous login requests.
prerequisites: RD Web Access must be deployed and accessible over the internet.
applicability_signals: RD Web Access login page is accessible.
skip_if: RD Web Access is not deployed or not publicly accessible.
Strong brute-force protections (e.g., account lockout) are in place.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify publicly accessible Microsoft RD Web Access login pages using search engines or network scanning tools.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send login requests to the RD Web Access login page with a valid username and a sequence of invalid passwords.
Step 2: Measure the response time for each login attempt.

Step 3: Analyze the response times to identify a statistically significant difference between successful authentication (valid username, correct password) and failed authentication attempts (valid username, incorrect password) due to timing variations.
payloads_or_patterns: Send POST requests to the RD Web Access login endpoint. Payload would be a username with a sequence of potentially incorrect passwords.
tools_recommended: Custom scripts or tools for sending numerous HTTP requests and measuring response times (e.g., Python with `requests` and `time` modules).
success_indicators: A measurable, consistent difference in response times when a correct password is used compared to incorrect passwords for a given username. This difference implies that the server performs additional, time-consuming operations upon successful authentication, or that incorrect password processing is faster.
failure_indicators: All login attempts result in the same response time, indicating no discernible timing difference. This suggests the timing attack is not applicable or mitigated. Pivot to other attack vectors if available.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to determine the correct password for a valid username by observing timing differences in authentication responses, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive systems via RD Web Access.
playbooks.key_lesson: When performing brute-force attacks against authentication mechanisms, measure and analyze response times for subtle differences that might indicate the success or failure of authentication, even without explicit error messages.

meta.title: CVE-2020-9759 - Getting root on webOS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application or service needs to download a file over the network using the Downloadmanager service, and the calling application does not have a privileged prefix (com.palm., com.webos., or com.palm.).
prerequisites: Ability to run a third-party webOS application or service.
applicability_signals: Presence of the Downloadmanager service
The download method within Downloadmanager does not properly sanitize the destination path and filename, allowing for arbitrary file writes.
LG claims this affects webOS version 5.0.0-88 but not devices with customized configurations.
skip_if: The target device has LG's custom configurations in place that restrict access to the vulnerable component.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target device is running a version of webOS that might be vulnerable (e.g., version 5.0.0-88).
Step 2: Attempt to deploy a test web app to the target webOS device to confirm it can interact with the Luna Service API and the Downloadmanager.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious webOS application that calls the Downloadmanager.download API.
Step 2: In the download API call, provide a crafted URL that points to a local file on the webOS system that the attacker wishes to overwrite (e.g., a system binary or configuration file).
Step 3: Provide a crafted destination path and filename that allows writing to an arbitrary location on the filesystem, overwriting a critical file.
Step 4: Execute the malicious webOS application, triggering the download and file overwrite.
tools_recommended: webOS TV SDK (for emulator testing)
Custom webOS application development environment
success_indicators: Arbitrary file overwrite confirmed by observing the modified file's content or behavior.
Achieving root privileges on the webOS device.
failure_indicators: Downloadmanager.download API call fails with an error related to unauthorized access or insufficient privileges.
The file is not overwritten or is overwritten with unexpected content.
The webOS device becomes unstable or unbootable due to incorrect file modification.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve local privilege escalation to root on the webOS device, allowing for full control over the system, execution of arbitrary commands, and access to sensitive data.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with internal services like Downloadmanager, always validate that the calling application has the necessary privileges and that input parameters (like destination paths) are properly sanitized to prevent arbitrary file writes and privilege escalation.

meta.title: 1st Facebook Bug Bounty | Disclose page’s admin to mod/admin of group

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook Page is linked to a Facebook Group, and the Page's admin/moderator role automatically grants access to the group without explicit approval. This allows an attacker to infer the identity of Page admins by observing group activity.
prerequisites: The target must be using Facebook Groups and Pages functionalities.
A Facebook Page must be linked to a Facebook Group.
The group must have settings that allow page admins/mods to join automatically without approval.
The attacker must be able to view the group's 'Activity Log' or 'Member Requests'.
applicability_signals: A Facebook Group displays an 'Activity Log' that can be accessed by moderators/admins.
A Facebook Page is visibly linked to a Facebook Group (e.g., on the Page's profile).
skip_if: Group settings require explicit admin approval for all new members, including those associated with linked pages.
The 'Activity Log' is not accessible to the attacker's privilege level.
The Page is not linked to any groups, or the linkage does not confer automatic group membership.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a Facebook Group that is linked to a Facebook Page. Look for direct links from the Page to the Group or vice-versa.

Step 2: Verify if the group's settings allow Page admins/mods to join automatically without a join request being approved. This might be inferred from the group's privacy settings or by observing existing members.

Step 3: Attempt to access the 'Activity Log' (e.g., www.facebook.com/groups/<GROUP_ID>/admin_activities) if you have moderator or admin privileges within the group. If not, look for ways to gain such privileges or observe public activity.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new Facebook Group and a new Facebook Page. Link the Page to the Group.
Step 2: Add a user as an 'analyst' (or similar role) to the Page. This user will represent the entity whose admin status is to be disclosed.
Step 3: Add another user as a 'mod' (or admin) to the Group. This user will monitor the group's activities.
Step 4: The 'analyst' user (associated with the Page) joins the group. If the group settings allow automatic joining for Page admins/mods, this user will be added without a visible join request.
Step 5: The 'mod' user monitors the 'Activity Log' and 'Member Requests' within the group.

Step 6: By observing the 'Activity Log' or 'Member Requests', the attacker can identify the user (UserB in the writeup) who joined automatically. This user is inferred to be an admin or moderator of the linked Page.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a behavioral exploit targeting settings and visibility, not a direct payload injection).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests/responses during group and page interactions, though not strictly necessary for this specific flow)
Facebook's own interface for managing Groups and Pages.
success_indicators: Observing the 'analyst' user (UserB) appearing in the group's 'Activity Log' or 'Member Requests' without having submitted a formal join request, while knowing they are an analyst of the linked Page.
Successfully identifying the specific user ID or profile of the Page admin/moderator through group activity logs.
failure_indicators: The 'analyst' user's join request is pending approval and requires explicit admin consent, indicating automatic joining is not enabled.
The 'Activity Log' does not show any unusual join activity that can be correlated with Page roles.
The Page is not linked to the group in a way that grants automatic membership.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of Page administrator or moderator identities to group moderators/administrators who might not otherwise have access to this information. This could lead to targeted harassment, social engineering, or further privilege escalation attempts.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a Page is linked to a Group, always check if the Group's membership approval settings allow automatic joining for Page roles, as this can inadvertently disclose Page admin identities through observable group activity logs.
